request.company_description: PeakHealth provides a clinical workflow automation platform for mid-size hospital systems and specialty clinics. Core product digitizes care coordination, referral management, and outcomes tracking. FDA-registered as a Class I medical device.
request.management_bios: CEO: Dr. Raj Patel — former Chief Medical Informatics Officer at Intermountain Health. CTO: Lisa Chang — ex-Epic Systems, 12 years in healthcare IT. VP Sales: Open role, currently managed by CEO.
request.known_competitors: Epic Systems, Cerner (Oracle Health), athenahealth, Innovaccer
request.customer_reviews: Strong clinical adoption but implementation timelines consistently run 2x longer than projected. Users praise the clinical workflow design but note limited reporting capabilities.

condensed.condensed_summary:
## Company Overview
PeakHealth is a healthcare SaaS platform targeting mid-size hospital systems (200-800 beds) and specialty clinic networks. Product digitizes care coordination, referral management, and outcomes tracking. FDA-registered as Class I medical device.

## Financial Profile
- ARR: $8M (40% YoY growth)
- NRR: 105% (modest expansion)
- GRR: 88% (below best-in-class)
- 145 customers at $55K ACV
- Gross Margin: 72%
- CAC Payback: 22 months
- LTV/CAC: 2.8x

## Management
Clinically-led team. CEO is former CMIO at Intermountain. CTO is ex-Epic with deep healthcare IT expertise. VP Sales role is OPEN — currently CEO-led sales.

## Competitive Landscape
Competes with Epic, Oracle Health (Cerner), athenahealth, and Innovaccer. Positioned as modern alternative for mid-market hospitals that can't afford Epic.

## Key Concerns
- GRR of 88% signals elevated churn
- No VP Sales — bottleneck to scaling
- Implementation timelines running 2x projected
- 72% gross margins may reflect heavy professional services
- Limited reporting capabilities noted in reviews
- FDA registration creates regulatory complexity

analysis.executive_summary: PeakHealth is a healthcare SaaS platform with $8M ARR growing 40% YoY, serving mid-size hospital systems with clinical workflow automation. While the company addresses a genuine market need and has strong clinical product design, several yellow flags warrant careful diligence: GRR of 88% suggests elevated churn, NRR of 105% indicates limited expansion motion, implementation timelines are running 2x projections, and the VP Sales role remains open. The regulatory moat (FDA Class I) is both a barrier to entry and a constraint on iteration speed.

company_overview.claim: Healthcare SaaS platform for mid-size hospital systems and specialty clinic networks
Core product: care coordination, referral management, outcomes tracking
FDA-registered as Class I medical device

business_model_description.claim: Significant professional services component for implementation (6-12 month cycles)
72% gross margins suggest meaningful services mix depressing software-only margins
Limited expansion revenue motion — 105% NRR vs. 125%+ best-in-class

market_analysis.tam: Healthcare IT market is $350B globally with clinical workflow automation representing ~$15B
market_analysis.sam: Mid-size US hospital systems (200-800 beds) clinical workflow software: ~$5B
market_analysis.som: Mid-market hospitals not yet committed to Epic/Cerner: ~$1.2B near-term addressable
market_analysis.methodology: KLAS Research healthcare IT market data combined with AHA hospital census data. ~2,000 mid-size hospitals × estimated $600K annual clinical software spend. SOM filtered for non-Epic/Cerner committed facilities.

why_now.claim: CMS value-based care mandates driving need for outcomes tracking and care coordination tools
Mid-size hospitals increasingly priced out of Epic — need modern alternatives
COVID exposed care coordination gaps across hospital systems
Interoperability mandates (21st Century Cures Act) creating opportunities for modern platforms

expansion_paths: Ambulatory care and outpatient clinic networks
Payer-provider data exchange and prior authorization
Outcomes analytics and benchmarking data product

competitive_positioning.name: Epic Systems
competitive_positioning.description: Dominant EHR provider. Near-monopoly in large hospital systems. Expanding into mid-market.
competitive_positioning.differentiation: PeakHealth is purpose-built for mid-market vs. Epic's enterprise-first approach. Lower TCO and faster time-to-value.

competitive_positioning.name: Oracle Health (Cerner)
competitive_positioning.description: Second largest EHR. Government contracts (VA). Uncertain product direction post-Oracle acquisition.
competitive_positioning.differentiation: Oracle's healthcare strategy is unclear. PeakHealth offers focused clinical workflow vs. broad but poorly integrated EHR.

competitive_positioning.name: athenahealth
competitive_positioning.description: Cloud-native platform for ambulatory and small hospital systems. Strong in billing/revenue cycle.
competitive_positioning.differentiation: PeakHealth has deeper clinical workflow capabilities while athenahealth is primarily revenue-cycle focused.

competitive_positioning.name: Innovaccer
competitive_positioning.description: Healthcare data platform. Well-funded ($200M+ raised). Strong in population health analytics.
competitive_positioning.differentiation: Innovaccer focuses on data unification and analytics while PeakHealth provides operational workflow automation.

arr.commentary: Modest ARR for healthcare — longer sales cycles typical. Path to $25M+ unclear without VP Sales.

arr_growth_yoy.commentary: Decent growth but below top-tier healthcare SaaS (Veeva grew 50%+ at similar stage). CEO-led sales is a constraint.

nrr.commentary: Below best-in-class. Limited expansion revenue suggests single-product usage without meaningful upsell motion.

grr.commentary: Below 90% threshold — 12% annual gross churn is elevated for enterprise healthcare SaaS. Investigate churn drivers.

gross_margin.commentary: Below SaaS benchmark — likely reflects heavy professional services. Need to decompose subscription vs. services margin.

cac_payback.commentary: On the higher end. Healthcare sales cycles are long but 22 months creates cash flow challenges at scale.

ltv_cac.commentary: Below 3x threshold. Combination of elevated churn and moderate expansion limits lifetime value.

rule_of_40.commentary: Assuming ~-10% FCF margin. Below Rule of 40, indicating growth-profitability balance needs improvement.

magic_number.commentary: Slightly below efficiency threshold. Suggests sales capacity constraints or long cycle inefficiency.

logo_retention.commentary: Borderline. 10% annual logo churn in healthcare where switching costs should be high is concerning.

revenue_quality_assessment.claim: 88% GRR indicates meaningful base erosion — need to understand churn drivers
Limited expansion motion (105% NRR) suggests single-product adoption without upsell path
Professional services revenue likely masking lower subscription margins
Annual contracts with healthcare systems provide some revenue predictability

unit_economics_assessment.claim: 22-month CAC payback is borderline for healthcare SaaS — acceptable given long enterprise cycles
2.8x LTV/CAC below 3x threshold — churn is destroying lifetime value
Implementation overruns likely increasing effective CAC beyond reported figures

growth_durability.claim: Large addressable market ($5B SAM) provides runway but execution risks are high
Without VP Sales, growth is bottlenecked by CEO bandwidth
Regulatory moat (FDA) protects from easy market entry but slows product iteration

strengths.claim: CEO Dr. Patel has deep clinical credibility as former CMIO at Intermountain — critical for hospital CXO sales
CTO's 12 years at Epic provides deep understanding of healthcare IT infrastructure and compliance

gaps: VP Sales role is OPEN — CEO-led sales is a critical bottleneck at $8M ARR
No VP Customer Success — may explain elevated churn (88% GRR)
No regulatory/compliance officer despite FDA registration
Implementation team appears under-resourced given 2x timeline overruns
management_assessment.scaling_readiness: Concerning. Clinical product excellence but critical go-to-market and customer success gaps. CEO cannot continue as primary seller beyond $10-12M ARR. Implementation capacity is a binding constraint.
management_assessment.key_person_risk: High. Dr. Patel is the clinical credibility, primary seller, and product visionary. Departure would significantly impact customer confidence in a market where physician trust is essential.

investment_thesis.thesis_one_liner: PeakHealth is a clinically-excellent workflow platform for underserved mid-market hospitals, but elevated churn and go-to-market gaps create execution risk that must be addressed pre-investment.

bull_case.claim: Genuine clinical product differentiation — designed by physicians, for physicians
Mid-market hospitals are underserved by Epic/Cerner — large captive demand
FDA registration creates meaningful barrier to entry for new competitors
Value-based care mandates create structural tailwind for outcomes tracking tools

bear_case.claim: 88% GRR signals product-market fit issues — customers are leaving at elevated rates
No VP Sales at $8M ARR — company has never built a scalable sales motion
Implementation timelines 2x projected — burns customer trust and cash
Epic's mid-market push could compress addressable market significantly

value_creation_levers: Hire VP Sales and build repeatable enterprise sales playbook
Invest in customer success to reduce churn from 12% to <8%
Standardize implementations to reduce timeline and improve margins
Build expansion products (analytics, population health) to drive NRR above 115%
Pursue SOC 2 Type II and additional certifications to accelerate enterprise sales
investment_thesis.what_makes_this_a_no: If churn is driven by fundamental product-market fit issues (not just implementation problems), and if Epic's mid-market push gains traction, PeakHealth could find itself squeezed between a dominant competitor moving downmarket and a shrinking customer base. The lack of a VP Sales at $8M ARR is a red flag that the company hasn't proven it can sell beyond the CEO's personal network.

risks.description: 88% GRR suggests customers are churning at elevated rates — may indicate product gaps
risks.mitigant: Conduct detailed churn analysis. If implementation-driven, fixable. If product-driven, much harder.
risks.diligence_question: What are the top 3 reasons customers have churned in the last 12 months?

risks.description: Epic expanding into mid-market with Community Connect and lighter-weight offerings
risks.mitigant: Epic's mid-market efforts historically underwhelm. True mid-market requires different GTM.
risks.diligence_question: How often do you compete with Epic in deals? What has changed in the last 12 months?

risks.description: No VP Sales — growth bottlenecked by CEO-led selling
risks.mitigant: Condition investment on hiring VP Sales within 90 days with proven healthcare sales leadership
risks.diligence_question: How many VP Sales candidates have been interviewed? Why is the role still open?

risks.description: Implementations running 2x projected timelines, burning customer trust
risks.mitigant: Invest in implementation playbooks, templated configurations, and dedicated implementation team
risks.diligence_question: What is the root cause of implementation delays? What is the planned fix?

risks.description: FDA Class I registration creates compliance burden and slows iteration
risks.mitigant: Establish dedicated regulatory function. FDA Class I is relatively light — ensure processes are in place.
risks.diligence_question: What is the current regulatory compliance process? How does it impact release cycles?

risks.description: High key-person risk on CEO for clinical credibility and sales
risks.mitigant: Build clinical advisory board and hire VP Sales to distribute relationship risk
risks.diligence_question: What percentage of customer relationships are CEO-dependent?

next_diligence_steps: Conduct detailed churn analysis — interview 10+ churned customers on reasons for leaving
Decompose gross margin into subscription vs. professional services components
Audit 5 recent implementations to understand root cause of timeline overruns
Interview VP Sales candidates to assess talent pool quality and timeline to hire
Validate FDA compliance processes and impact on product development velocity
Conduct 15 customer reference calls focusing on implementation experience and product gaps
Assess Epic's mid-market strategy and competitive positioning in detail
Review product roadmap for expansion revenue opportunities (analytics, pop health)
Model scenario: what if churn increases to 15%+ due to Epic competitive pressure
Evaluate build vs. buy options for implementation acceleration
suggested_data_requests: Detailed churn log with reasons, ARR impact, and customer characteristics
Gross margin decomposition: subscription, professional services, support
Implementation timeline data: projected vs. actual for all customers
Sales pipeline detail with stage duration and conversion rates
Customer NPS/CSAT scores segmented by implementation vintage
Product usage analytics: DAU/MAU, feature adoption rates
Engineering velocity metrics: sprint points, release frequency
Competitive win/loss data with detailed reasons
Customer contract terms: length, auto-renewal, termination clauses
Organizational chart with all roles, tenure, and open positions

memo_markdown:
# Investment Committee Memorandum
## PeakHealth — Healthcare SaaS Clinical Workflow Platform

**Date**: March 2026 | **Sector**: HealthTech / Vertical SaaS | **Stage**: Growth Equity

---

## Executive Summary

PeakHealth is a healthcare SaaS platform with $8M ARR growing 40% YoY, serving mid-size hospital systems with clinical workflow automation. While the company addresses a genuine market need with strong clinical product design, several yellow flags require careful diligence: 88% GRR (elevated churn), 105% NRR (limited expansion), implementations running 2x projected timelines, and a critical VP Sales vacancy. The investment case hinges on whether these are fixable execution issues or structural product-market fit problems.

**Investment Thesis**: PeakHealth is a clinically-excellent workflow platform for underserved mid-market hospitals, but elevated churn and go-to-market gaps create execution risk that must be addressed pre-investment.

---

## Company Overview

- Healthcare SaaS platform for mid-size hospital systems (200-800 beds)
- Core product: care coordination, referral management, outcomes tracking
- FDA-registered as Class I medical device
- 145 customers at $55K ACV

---

## Market Analysis

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| TAM | $15B — Clinical workflow automation |
| SAM | $5B — Mid-size US hospital systems |
| SOM | $1.2B — Non-Epic/Cerner committed facilities |

---

## Financial Analysis — SaaS Metrics

| Metric | Value | Benchmark | Rating |
|--------|-------|-----------|--------|
| ARR | $8.0M | Early growth stage | 🟡 |
| ARR Growth (YoY) | 40% | Good: 30-50% | 🟡 |
| Net Revenue Retention | 105% | Below best-in-class (>120%) | 🟡 |
| Gross Revenue Retention | 88% | Below threshold (>90%) | 🟡 |
| Gross Margin | 72% | Below SaaS benchmark (75-85%) | 🟡 |
| CAC Payback | 22 months | Borderline (18-24mo) | 🟡 |
| LTV/CAC | 2.8x | Below 3x threshold | 🟡 |
| Rule of 40 | ~30% | Below 40% target | 🟡 |

---

## Key Risks

| Risk | Severity | Likelihood |
|------|----------|------------|
| Product-market fit (88% GRR) | 4/5 | 3/5 |
| Epic mid-market expansion | 5/5 | 3/5 |
| No VP Sales | 4/5 | 4/5 |
| Implementation delays (2x) | 3/5 | 4/5 |
| Regulatory burden | 3/5 | 3/5 |
| Key person risk (CEO) | 4/5 | 2/5 |

> **What Would Make This a No**: If churn is product-driven rather than implementation-driven, and Epic's mid-market push gains real traction, PeakHealth could be squeezed between a dominant competitor and a shrinking base — at a scale that doesn't support profitability.

---

*Confidential — Prepared by Traverse Diligence Copilot*
